questions.question: Explain the classification of data structures into Primitive and Non-Primitive types with examples.
keyPoints: Primitive data structures are basic, fundamental types directly operated by machine instructions
Include: int, float, char, pointer
Non-Primitive are derived from primitive types and include Arrays, Linked Lists, Stacks, Queues, Trees, Graphs
Non-Primitive can be Linear (Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue) or Non-Linear (Tree, Graph)

questions.question: Describe the four fundamental operations performed on data structures.
keyPoints: Traversing: Accessing each element exactly once
Searching: Finding the location of a specific element
Inserting: Adding new elements at appropriate positions
Deleting: Removing existing elements from the structure

questions.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic memory allocation? Give examples.
keyPoints: Static allocation happens at compile time with fixed size known beforehand
Examples: arrays with fixed size, declared variables
Dynamic allocation happens at runtime using pointers and allocation functions
Functions like malloc(), calloc(), realloc() and free() in C are used for dynamic allocation
Dynamic allocation allows memory to grow/shrink during program execution

questions.question: Explain how a polynomial is represented using arrays with an example.
keyPoints: Array index represents the exponent of the variable
Array value at that index represents the coefficient
For polynomial a_n x^n + a_(n-1) x^(n-1) + ... + a_1 x + a_0
Array size = highest degree + 1
Example: 5x³ + 2x + 1 is stored as [1, 2, 0, 5]

questions.question: What is a Sparse Matrix? Explain any one method of representing it efficiently.
keyPoints: Sparse matrix: majority of elements are zero
Only non-zero elements are stored to save memory
COO (Coordinate) format stores triplets: (row, column, value)
CSR (Compressed Sparse Row) stores values, column indices, and row pointer array
Reduces space complexity from O(m×n) to O(k) where k = non-zero elements

questions.question: Differentiate between Structures and Unions in C programming.
keyPoints: Structures allocate separate memory for each member
Unions allocate shared memory for all members (size of largest member)
In unions, only one member can hold a value at a time
Structures are used when different types of data need to be grouped
Unions are used when only one of several data types will be used at a time

questions.question: Explain Row-Major and Column-Major ordering for storing 2D arrays in memory.
keyPoints: Both store multidimensional arrays as one-dimensional in memory
Row-Major: elements of each row stored consecutively, then next row (C uses this)
Column-Major: elements of each column stored consecutively, then next column (Fortran uses this)
Address formula in row-major: Base + (i × n + j) × elementSize
Address formula in column-major: Base + (j × m + i) × elementSize

questions.question: Write a C program to dynamically allocate a 1D array, accept elements, and display them. Also explain the use of malloc() and free() functions.
keyPoints: Use malloc() to allocate memory at runtime
malloc() returns void pointer, cast to appropriate type
Check for NULL after allocation
Accept elements using loop
Display elements
Use free() to deallocate memory after use
Explain memory leak prevention

questions.question: Explain the concept of dynamic memory allocation for arrays. Write pseudocode to create a dynamic array that can grow as needed.
keyPoints: Dynamic arrays use pointers and allocation functions
Initial capacity is set, when full, new larger array is allocated
Copy existing elements to new array
Deallocate old array using free()
Update pointer and capacity
This is the principle behind vector in C++ and ArrayList in Java

questions.question: Describe the different operations that can be performed on Arrays with their time complexities.
keyPoints: Access: O(1) using index - direct calculation
Search: O(n) for linear search, O(log n) for binary search (if sorted)
Insert: O(n) - may require shifting elements
Delete: O(n) - may require shifting elements
Traverse: O(n) - visit each element once
Update: O(1) - modify element at given index

questions.question: Explain how Structures are used to represent a polynomial and compare it with array representation.
keyPoints: Structure can store non-zero terms with coefficient and exponent
More memory efficient for sparse polynomials (few non-zero terms)
Array representation simpler but wastes space for sparse polynomials
Structure approach: define struct Term {int coeff; int exp;}
Create array of Term structures for polynomial
Trade-off: more complex but efficient for sparse polynomials
